Transcrição Completa: 13 Before You Even Think About Getting A Partner

 

[00:00][music]

 

[00:01]So, when you're first getting started, having a business partner can be one of the worst decisions that you can make, especially when you're a noob or a novice.

 

[00:10]That's because you don't know what you're doing. You don't know the game. You're not aware of what a business partner really brings to the table for you.

 

[00:18]And this can be a horrible situation to find yourself in given things go bad. Maybe you have to buy them out.

 

[00:24]Maybe you said, "Hey, we'll do 50/50 on the agency." But you found out 50% of the money from your agency is not enough money.

 

[00:33]Maybe you found out after a few months of having a business partner and as you continued to become more successful, giving better habits, reading more, self-educating more, and just overall bringing more to the table that that business partner didn't do any of that while you are being more successful.

 

[00:50]And now you got to cut somebody out who unfortunately has a percentage of your business. So, you're going to have to buy that person out.

 

[00:58]You might even have to go into the courts here. You might have to pay out some lawyer costs. And it is overall a very novice mistake, especially when you're first getting started.

 

[01:08]At different levels of the game, I usually find this in people who are much more mature, even to myself currently at the age of 24, 25.

 

[01:17]These people are in their late 20s, early 30s. That's when they choose to start getting business partners.

 

[01:24]After they've had a few businesses, they understand their strengths and weaknesses acutely. They're very self-aware and they say to themselves, you know, I have these strengths, but I also have these weaknesses.

 

[01:37]This business partner I could strategically bring in their strengths cover my weaknesses and my strengths cover their weaknesses. That'd be a strategic business partnership.

 

[01:47]But you have to once again be very self-aware of if a business partner is even needed.

 

[01:52]A percentage of your business and sharing profits is a very large responsibility and a very large amount of money that you're going to be giving out.

 

[02:00]And also it's a lot of liability to have legally on paper.

 

[02:04]So to be very clear here and very direct with you if you're just getting started, especially if you're just getting started in business as a whole, do not jump into business and have a business partner right away.

 

[02:17]Because most of the things that you're going to find you think you need a business partner for, it can be outsourced to a contractor for hundreds of dollars, sometimes even thousands.

 

[02:27]Comparatively to a percentage of all the profits that come in from your business.

 

[02:32]So be very self-aware of this. Be very self-aware if you are going to have a business partner of the other person's habits.

 

[02:40]Make sure that you're very strict on your standards when you're with a business partner. It's not a regular friendship.

 

[02:47]You know, when you have a business partner, you want to make sure you can communicate effectively and properly.

 

[02:52]You want to make sure you have a great working relationship. You want to make sure there's not going to be any tension between you and this other person.

 

[03:00]And that if there ever is, there can in a healthy way be communicated and deescalated.

 

[03:06]If you are at any point in the game going to go in to business with a business partner, do the math on how much you are going to make and how much the other person's going to make.

 

[03:16]After all of your expenses, taxes, liability, business operating cost, and just general savings for the business is set aside.

 

[03:24]Is it worth it for you? Is it going to be worth all the time, energy, attention?

 

[03:29]Or is it going to be worth the time, energy, and attention to simply invest into yourself to build the skills in the areas where you're currently weak.

 

[03:38]To then see if it's a true weakness or not, or if you just needed the education in that area.

 

[03:44]I find most of the amateur-ish, more novice, beginner students that come into this program, they have business partners for all the wrong reasons.

 

[03:53]They have business partners because they're lonely because they think that it will give them more confidence or help them in business from thinking that a business partner does those things and handles the magic part of the business they don't understand.

 

[04:07]You have to be aware of something to manage something and to keep something accountable.

 

[04:12]Don't sign away a portion of your business to somebody else who might end up being one of the biggest liabilities you've ever had when you're first getting started with your marketing agency.

 

[04:22]I'll see you in the next video.

 

 

📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO

 

NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS:

50/50 split mencionado como exemplo problemático

Idade do palestrante: 24-25 anos

Faixa etária recomendada para parceria: final dos 20s, início dos 30s

Custo de terceirização: centenas a milhares de dólares vs. porcentagem perpétua dos lucros

 

ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:

Evitar parceria no início: Não ter sócios quando começando do zero

Terceirização estratégica: Contratar serviços específicos ao invés de dar equity

Auto-desenvolvimento primeiro: Investir em construir habilidades próprias antes de buscar parceiro

Análise financeira pré-parceria: Calcular lucros líquidos após despesas, impostos, custos operacionais e reservas

Parceria estratégica madura: Forças complementares que cobrem fraquezas mútuas

 

ESTRUTURA:

Framework de autoconhecimento: Identificar forças e fraquezas antes de considerar parceria

Critérios de avaliação de parceiro: Hábitos, padrões de trabalho, capacidade de comunicação

Sistema de relacionamento profissional: Diferente de amizade, requer comunicação efetiva e gestão de conflitos

Estrutura de decisão: Avaliar se é fraqueza real ou falta de educação na área

 

DADOS FINANCEIROS:

Custos de terceirização: centenas a milhares de dólares (one-time ou recorrente)

Custo de parceria: porcentagem perpétua de todos os lucros

Custos potenciais de saída: buyout do parceiro, processos judiciais, honorários advocatícios

Impacto financeiro: 50% dos lucros pode não ser suficiente para o fundador principal

 

RISCOS IDENTIFICADOS:

Desalinhamento de crescimento e desenvolvimento pessoal

Necessidade de buyout e custos legais

Parceiro como passivo ao invés de ativo

Motivações erradas: solidão, falta de confiança, incompreensão do negócio

 

DURAÇÃO: 04:25 FORMATO: Vídeo educacional / Aula de programa de agência de marketing
